transcript.clean_text: - We've made it here to Málaga for the start of the 2018 Vuelta a España, and we've gone to the top of Mount Gibralfaro to take a birds' eye look at the eight-kilometer TT course. This is a fast-flowing course and will take in some of Málaga's most famous and iconic landmarks. Let's go and take a close look at the start off the promenade and down the pier. The riders will roll down the ramp just over there in the center of Pompidou Málaga. They'll then head right down that pier all the way to the end, where they'll do a U-turn. Pretty night place to start. Now, I've got a big headwind here, so getting nice and narrow is gonna play a really important part in getting a really fast time. Once the riders get here, at the end of the pier, they hear their first obstacle. That's a U-turn around this roundabout. Now, what I would do is go into the corner as fast as possible, leave it late on the brakes, sweep around the corner and then back on the gas, trying to keep as much momentum as possible. I mean, this is where the fast corners are really gonna make some time up. The riders will be up speed, hitting speeds of up to 60 kilometers an hour. They'll fly past the La Farola Lighthouse. It's one of Spain's oldest lighthouses and stands 38 meters above sea level. After 4.2 kilometers, they get here. It's then a left-hander up the Paseo Salvador Rueda, which is 1.1 kilometers long at 5.5%. The riders then hit this climb. It's a big ring climb, but is it gonna be enough to benefit the smaller riders like Richie Porte? I mean, we'll have to see. This climb is overlooked by the Castillo de Gibralfaro. I think we should take a closer look. We have made it to the summit of the Castillo de Gibralfaro. Now, this sits right on top of the city of Málaga and it's got an incredible view over the port. It dates back to to the 10th century, and it's famous for the three-month siege that the Catholics did, which only ended in hunger. Now, before I head down the mountain to look at the rest of the stage, interesting fact for you, it was the first siege that gunpowder was used by both sides. Now, is this stage gonna be that explosive? Well, we'll have to see won't we? After the one-kilometer climb, they arrive here at the summit. It's now into the 11 and it's a fast downhill. It's gonna be exciting. Who's gonna take the most risks? We'll have to see. Once the riders have gone through the tunnel and taken the right-hander, which takes them past this magnificent fountain and onto the Paseo del Parque. With three K to go, the riders pass these amazing gardens of Pedro Luis Alonso. The riders also pass Málaga's city hall. This is one of the most picturesque city halls. The riders will then come down here. What an incredible backdrop, the Málaga Cathedral. Wowzers! Well, the last kilometer is fairly technical and you got this polished finish so is that gonna make a difference? I mean, hopefully they got good tires, but they're gonna be coming down here fast for the finish. After eight kilometers of the fast course, they arrive here in the center of the plaza for the finish of the first stage of the Vuelta a España. Who's gonna be in the red jersey? Well, my bet is Rohan Dennis, but we'll see tomorrow. I hope this has given you an insight of the first stage of the Vuelta a España. If you did like this video, then give us a thumbs up. And for more of Vuelta action, click down here.
